text: This video presents a laparoscopic varietal amycolectomy for CT-IV-B cecal adenocarcinoma involving the bladder. Patient is a forty-four-year-old woman with a five-month history of abdominal pain, diarrhea, and a positive vesicle occult blood test. Vesicle adenocarcinoma was found at endoscopic examination. Imaging doesn't show any evidence of metastatic disease. The cecum was placed towards the midline and the detective cleavage plane with the bladder was not clear. Lab tests found anemia and an increased value of CA-. The placement of our trochers is shown here.

The surgeon identifies the ileocolic pedicle and incises the peritoneum, using lateral traction on the ileocecal junction. The ileocolic vessels are identified at their origin, with the vein and artery dissected separately and ligated. The origin of the ileocolic vein and the superior mesenteric vein are fully exposed.

The surgeon identifies the duodenum and uses a medial to lateral approach to develop the mesenteric window. Dissection is performed along the embryological avascular plane, separating the posterior mesocolic layer from the retroperitoneal space. VLA maneuvers facilitate dissection between the toes and gyrosis fascia cranially, with sharp dissection used for lateral isolation of the duodenum. Medially, the submesocolic area on the head of the pancreas is exposed. Suspicious malignant central nodes are detected and dissected along the anterior aspect of the superior mesenteric vein.
The surgeon identifies and ligates the superior right colic vein. Cranially, the colipiploic detachment is performed, visibly separating the omentum from the transverse mesocolon.

The surgeon mobilizes the hepatic flexure by sectioning the peritoneal attachments, revealing the previous surgical plane. The dissection proceeds from top to bottom, including the peritoneum around the cecum. The cancerous tissue involving the bladder dome is addressed with a partial cystectomy, resulting in an oblique tumor resection.

The surgeon performs a reconstruction using a double-layer suture covered by a peritoneal flap. The superior mesenteric vein and vascular ties are visible following the lymphadenectomy. A side-to-side intracorporeal ileocolic anastomosis is conducted, starting with a single stitch at the lower corner. The enterotomy is closed with a double-layer technique using a running barb suture for the first layer.

No complications occurred during the hospital stay. Urinary catheter was removed in the fourteenth postoperative day and final pathology stage at T for A and one B and one purely differentiated sickle adenocarcinoma.
